observation: Keyword search returned a long list of email-marketing, pricing-plan, billing, and unrelated plan files.
why_it_mattered: The query words were common across the corpus, so the agent had to manually separate pricing intent from adjacent usage and quota articles.

command: rg -i "Email Marketing pricing plans|pricing plans|compare the different email marketing plans|pricing" corpus/wixqa/articles/Email_Marketing* -n
observation: The agent narrowed to email-marketing files, but still saw several plausible pages about free usage, monthly balance, upgrades, and getting started.
why_it_mattered: Exact matching helped reduce the set, but it did not clearly rank the article that answered pricing.

observation: The monthly-balance article contained plan and quota language, which looked relevant to pricing.
why_it_mattered: This was the wrong intent: it explained monthly email balance rather than which articles answer Wix email marketing pricing.

observation: The baseline selected the balance/quota article.
why_it_mattered: It missed both expected articles even after 11 shell commands and 93,188 token usage.

command: mfs search "pricing for email marketing on Wix" corpus/wixqa --top-k 20
observation: Semantic search surfaced articles about free email marketing, upgrading an email marketing plan, and campaign setup.
why_it_mattered: The candidate set aligned with the user's pricing intent instead of only repeated keywords.

observation: The upgrade-plan outline directly matched paid email marketing plans.
why_it_mattered: A compact peek was enough to distinguish plan pricing from monthly-balance usage.

command: mfs search "200 emails a month for free upgrade email marketing plan pricing" corpus/wixqa --top-k 10
observation: The refined semantic query connected free monthly quota, upgrade language, and campaign creation guidance.
why_it_mattered: This led the agent to include the second expected article rather than stopping at a single nearby page.

observation: The combined workflow returned both expected articles.
why_it_mattered: It found the complete answer with 35,783 token usage, about 62% lower than the shell-only run.
